realization_fidelity.flattened_to: The implementation is a Riemann-sum sampling game where students click to place sample points on a curve and see an estimated area accumulate, rather than the predict-then-measure betting interaction where students first commit a numerical prediction token, then watch liquid fill to reveal the true area.
violations: manipulated_variable: Students manipulate sample point placement (clicks on curve) and bound positions (dragging handles), NOT a numerical area prediction value typed/dialed before revelation

primary_visual_feedback: No prediction token locks in place; no liquid-fill animation from bottom-up with live counter climbing from zero to true area; instead shows Riemann-sum rectangles accumulating as samples are placed
goal_structure: No predict-then-reveal commit phase; students freely click to sample without budgeting a prediction first, then see estimated vs. true distance (not area under curve animated fill)

causal_chain_exposed: Running counter and fill dynamics show Riemann sum convergence, not the visual correlation between curve height at each position and area contribution via animated liquid displacement
trace_strategy: No mention of faint ghost tokens with error bars tracking prediction history across trials; instead focuses on sample point accumulation

aha_moment: The insight about tall-narrow vs. wide-short sections and concavity patterns is not naturally emergent from the betting-and-fill mechanic; the design invites calibration through sampling, which is a different cognitive path

polish.worst_offense: The speedometer icon inside the teal circle violates the no-emoji/clip-art rule and adds unnecessary visual noise to an otherwise clean concept representation.
fixes: Remove the speedometer emoji icon from the teal circle on the curve; replace with a simple dot or crosshair if a marker is needed to highlight that sampling point.
The 'Reveal true distance' button is visually quiet and could be mistaken for non-interactive; apply the accent teal color and add subtle hover affordance (scale/shadow) to signal clickability.
Reduce the visual weight of the vertical dashed lines (a and b handles) slightly—they compete with the curve accent; use the lighter ink gray (#94A3B8) instead of the current darker gray.

results.description: On load, the shaded region is visible but area is not revealed - posing the challenge of estimating area under a curve
results.visual_check: shaded region visible between bounds, no fill animation running, area value hidden or showing placeholder

visual_verdict.observed: The two screenshots appear identical with no visible changes in the shaded region, fill animation, or area value display.

results.description: The left bound (a) can be dragged along the x-axis
results.visual_check: left bound handle moves to x=2, shaded region shrinks from left

visual_verdict.observed: The left bound handle (a) moved from x=0 to x=2, and the shaded region between the two handles visibly shrunk from the left side.

results.description: The right bound (b) can be dragged along the x-axis
results.visual_check: right bound handle moves to x=4, shaded region shrinks from right

visual_verdict.observed: The purple (b) handle moved leftward from x=8 to x=4, and the shaded region between the two handles correspondingly shrunk from the right side.

results.description: Dragging bounds causes the shaded region and area value to update in real-time
results.visual_check: shaded region expands to cover more area under curve, area readout increases

visual_verdict.observed: The purple dashed line (point b) moved from approximately 4 seconds to 8 seconds, expanding the shaded region under the curve to cover the entire time period shown.

results.description: Clicking on the timeline to sample speed costs one fuel token
results.visual_check: a sample marker appears at the clicked x-position, fuel token counter decreases by 1

visual_verdict.observed: A sample marker (airplane icon) appeared on the timeline at approximately t=4 seconds, and the fuel token counter decreased from 0/8 to 1/8 samples used.
